Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.",
  "opinion": "Well, I'm an old man living in Milan as a fitness trainer, though honestly I didn't have much formal schooling. Being widowed means I don't have that wife's input anymore; she was always the one guiding me through tough times like this vaccine discussion. Now, with Buddhism and being somewhat depressed, I find myself hesitant around big topics—especially when they're as divisive as COVID vaccines are now.\n\nI remember back in the day working out meant a lot to me, just getting fit without all these debates about germs or shots. But times have changed; people are talking everywhere, even on social media which I touch only sparingly because it makes my depression worse already thinking too much. So managing discussions? That’s tough for someone like me with high neuroticism but low agreeableness—wait no, actually from the scores I'm supposed to be moderate agreeable and high extraverted? Doesn't matter really.\n\nI think people need to listen more, not shout louder about vaccines or anything else. My depression means I’m already feeling overwhelmed by life’s uncertainties; healthcare topics just pile on with their talk of mutations and mandates. But then again, as an older man who used to lift weights for fun—if you ask me now though... yeah.\n\nThere's that rock climbing hobby too, which requires focus but also trust between partners, much like handling vaccine discussions carefully without falling into despair myself or giving unsolicited advice.",
  "reasoning_summary": "I considered my limited education and the absence of a spouse (who would provide guidance), then reflected on how Buddhism promotes calm discussion while my depression makes such topics emotionally taxing. My high extraversion means I used to enjoy debate but now, due to neuroticism, prefer less confrontational interactions.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
Well, I'm an old man living in Milan as a fitness trainer, though honestly I didn't have much formal schooling. Being widowed means I don't have that wife's input anymore; she was always the one guiding me through tough times like this vaccine discussion. Now, with Buddhism and being somewhat depressed, I find myself hesitant around big topics—especially when they're as divisive as COVID vaccines are now.

I remember back in the day working out meant a lot to me, just getting fit without all these debates about germs or shots. But times have changed; people are talking everywhere, even on social media which I touch only sparingly because it makes my depression worse already thinking too much. So managing discussions? That’s tough for someone like me with high neuroticism but low agreeableness—wait no, actually from the scores I'm supposed to be moderate agreeable and high extraverted? Doesn't matter really.

I think people need to listen more, not shout louder about vaccines or anything else. My depression means I’m already feeling overwhelmed by life’s uncertainties; healthcare topics just pile on with their talk of mutations and mandates. But then again, as an older man who used to lift weights for fun—if you ask me now though... yeah.

There's that rock climbing hobby too, which requires focus but also trust between partners, much like handling vaccine discussions carefully without falling into despair myself or giving unsolicited advice.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I considered my limited education and the absence of a spouse (who would provide guidance), then reflected on how Buddhism promotes calm discussion while my depression makes such topics emotionally taxing. My high extraversion means I used to enjoy debate but now, due to neuroticism, prefer less confrontational interactions.
response_parsed.tone: Reflective yet cautious, with underlying concern for others' mental well-being
